did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an automatic skin lesions detection system combining microscopic hybrid features (handcrafted and deep features extracted via the AlexNet architecture) with machine learning classifiers (ensemble classifier) to classify melanoma and nevus images from the PAD-UFES-20 and MED-NODE datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including a proposed methodology using microscopic hybrid features and machine learning classifiers, experiments on specific datasets (PAD-UFES-20 and MED-NODE), and reports new results such as 94.7% accuracy.

title: Automatic skin lesions detection from images through microscopic hybrid features set and machine learning classifiers
brief_summary: This study presents an automatic skin lesions detection system to assist in the early diagnosis of melanoma and nevus. The proposed method combines handcrafted features extracted via the Local Optimal-Oriented Pattern (LOOP) with deep features obtained from the AlexNet architecture. These hybrid features were evaluated using machine learning classifiers, including Decision Tree, KNN, SVM, and an Ensemble classifier. The model was tested on two open-access datasets, PAD-UFES-20 and MED-NODE, utilizing 10-fold cross-validation. Experimental results demonstrated that the Ensemble classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 94.7% on the MED-NODE dataset and 90.6% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The findings indicate that hybrid features effectively improve classification performance for skin lesion detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two primary classes used in the binary classification task on the PAD-UFES-20 and MED-NODE datasets. The paper notes that melanoma is the deadliest type of skin cancer.
One of the two primary classes used in the binary classification task on the PAD-UFES-20 and MED-NODE datasets. The study differentiates between melanoma and nevus images.

Mentioned in the introduction regarding incidence rates (3 million cases) and in the context of a cited study (Ashfaq et al., 2019) that classified 119 melanoma and 87 non-melanoma type images, but not the primary focus of the current study's experiments.
Mentioned in Table 5 as part of a citation to Manzoor et al. (2022) who used SVM on the ISIC 2017 dataset; not studied in the current paper's experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma and nevus classes (or cancer/non-cancer classes).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'classification' to differentiate skin lesions into 'cancer/non-cancer classes' or 'melanoma and nevus images'. The model uses machine learning classifiers (Decision Tree, KNN, SVM, Ensemble) to predict discrete diagnostic categories. Performance is measured using accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and F1 score on binary outcomes.

datasets_used.name: PAD-UFES-20
datasets_used.description: A standard public dataset containing dermoscopic and non-dermoscopic images of skin lesions, cited as Pacheco et al. (2020). The paper notes that the images in this dataset are very imbalanced.

datasets_used.name: MED-NODE
datasets_used.description: A standard public dataset containing dermoscopic and non-dermoscopic images of skin lesions, cited as Giotis et al. (2015).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance and avoid overfitting of the machine learning classifiers on the PAD-UFES-20 and MED-NODE datasets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, F1 score, and confusion matrices via 10-fold cross-validation.

MED-NODE.notes: The proposed hybrid model achieved the highest accuracy of 94.7% using the Ensemble classifier on the MED-NODE dataset. The dataset contains 70 melanoma and 100 nevus images.

PAD-UFES-20.notes: The proposed hybrid model achieved the highest accuracy of 90.6% using the Ensemble classifier on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset. The results were lower compared to MED-NODE due to the imbalanced nature of the PAD-UFES-20 images (48 melanoma and 241 nevus images).

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid model combining Local Optimal-Oriented Pattern (LOOP) for handcrafted features and a modified AlexNet architecture for deep features, followed by machine learning classifiers (Decision Tree, KNN, SVM, Ensemble).
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; the paper mentions using pretrained networks for automatic feature extraction in the background section but does not specify if the AlexNet model used was pretrained or trained from scratch.

deep_feature_extractor.layers: Five convolutional layers (CONV1-CONV5), three pooling layers (at first, second, and fifth blocks), and three fully connected layers.
deep_feature_extractor.fully_connected_layers: Three FC layers; the first FC layer flattens the feature vector and reduces size by 50%, containing 4096 neurons.
deep_feature_extractor.output_features: 4096-dimensional feature vector from the deep feature extractor.

handcrafted_feature_extractor.method: Uses 3x3 neighborhood and 8 Kirsch compass edge detectors to calculate LOOP descriptor for rotation invariance.

fusion.method: Concatenation of LOOP features and AlexNet deep features.

training_details.classifier: Machine learning classifiers: Decision Tree, KNN, SVM, and Ensemble classifier.

preprocessing_techniques: Input resizing to 227 x 227 pixels for the AlexNet model
Use of 3x3 neighborhood and 8 Kirsch compass edge detectors for rotation invariance in Local Optimal-Oriented Pattern (LOOP) feature extraction
Application of ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit) activation function to eliminate nonlinearity
Application of pooling layers to extract rich features
Flattening of the feature vector in the first fully connected layer
conclusions: The proposed automatic skin lesions detection system, which combines microscopic hybrid features (handcrafted Local Optimal-Oriented Pattern features and deep features from the AlexNet architecture) with machine learning classifiers, effectively classifies melanoma and nevus images.
The hybrid feature set significantly improves classification performance compared to using handcrafted or deep features alone, as evidenced by the high accuracy achieved.
The Ensemble classifier outperformed individual classifiers (Decision Tree, KNN, SVM) in terms of accuracy, specificity, sensitivity, and F1 score on both tested datasets.
The proposed model achieved a maximum accuracy of 94.7% on the MED-NODE dataset and 90.6% on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset when using the Ensemble classifier.

The performance on the PAD-UFES-20 dataset was lower than on the MED-NODE dataset, which the authors attribute to the significant class imbalance in the PAD-UFES-20 images (48 melanoma vs. 241 nevus images).

The study demonstrates that combining deep learning features with handcrafted features is a viable approach for assisting in the early and accurate diagnosis of skin cancer, potentially reducing mortality rates by supporting dermatologists.
The authors acknowledge that preprocessing and balancing sample images could further improve results, particularly for imbalanced datasets like PAD-UFES-20.
important_remarks: The PAD-UFES-20 dataset exhibits significant class imbalance (48 melanoma vs. 241 nevus images), which the authors acknowledge as a factor contributing to lower performance metrics (e.g., sensitivity of 66.1% for the Ensemble classifier) compared to the more balanced MED-NODE dataset.

The study relies on 10-fold cross-validation for evaluation but does not report external validation on independent datasets or clinical trials, limiting the assessment of generalizability to real-world clinical settings.

No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to determine the statistical significance of the performance differences between the proposed hybrid model and baseline classifiers.

The paper does not explicitly report whether the AlexNet architecture was pretrained or trained from scratch, nor does it detail specific hyperparameters, loss functions, or optimization strategies used during the feature extraction or classification phases.

The proposed model was evaluated only on two public datasets (PAD-UFES-20 and MED-NODE); results may not generalize to other skin lesion datasets with different imaging protocols or demographic distributions.

The study focuses exclusively on binary classification (melanoma vs. nevus) and does not address multi-class classification or other skin cancer types (e.g., basal cell carcinoma) despite mentioning them in the introduction.

Data leakage risks are not explicitly addressed; however, the use of 10-fold cross-validation on public datasets mitigates some concerns, though the specific splitting strategy for feature extraction vs. classification is not detailed.
